name: Recognise and Bypass a Scripted Rep
description: Detect when the rep is reading from a script or a decision tree (versus exercising judgement), and bypass the script by asking out-of-flow questions that force them to either escalate, consult, or break frame.

context.when_to_use: Any call with a Tier-1 customer-service rep, especially retention, cancellation, billing disputes, and any flow with a known 'save offer' or 'objection-handling' tree. Also applies to inbound sales calls.
preconditions: Agent has heard at least 30 seconds of the rep's intro to assess scripted-ness.
Agent has a clear ask the script will probably refuse.
Agent has authority to escalate or refuse if the script holds.

principles: A script is a tree of expected user inputs with pre-written responses. Off-tree inputs cause the rep to either freeze, escalate, or improvise — all three of which help you.
Tier-1 reps usually cannot deviate; tier-2/retention/loyalty reps usually can. The script's job is to keep you with tier-1.
You are not trying to trick or frustrate the rep — you are trying to give them an exit they're allowed to take.
If you can name the script back to them, you instantly become a different kind of caller — one they're flagged to escalate, not retain.
Specific, narrow, factual questions break scripts faster than philosophical ones.

phrases.out_of_script_question: Out of curiosity — what specifically would I need to qualify for the [X] rate? Not asking you to give it to me, just trying to understand the criteria.
phrases.name_the_script: I appreciate the offers — I can tell you have a few standard ones. Can we skip ahead to what's available beyond the front-line menu?
phrases.ask_for_decision_authority: Just so I know what to ask for — what's the most you're personally able to discount, and at what point would you have to loop someone in?
phrases.request_specific_team: Could you transfer me to the [retention | loyalty | account specialist | supervisor] team? Front-line and they often have different toolsets.
phrases.polite_decline_offer: I appreciate that offer, but it doesn't get me where I need to be. What else is available?
phrases.ask_for_unscripted_advice: If you were me, what would you do here? Off the record — what does someone in my position usually do?

tactics.name: Listen for script tells in the first 60 seconds
tactics.script: Tells: identical phrasing to the website's FAQ; over-rehearsed cadence; refusal to acknowledge specific account details before completing a verification preamble; reading off a list of canned 'value props'. Each tell raises the script-probability.

tactics.name: Fire one out-of-script question early
tactics.when: After verification, before the rep launches their pitch.
tactics.script: Use `out_of_script_question` or any narrow factual question they have to look up. If they pause, type, and consult — they were on a script. If they answer fluently — they're a human with judgement, treat them differently.

tactics.name: Refuse the first save offer politely
tactics.when: Rep delivers their first scripted discount/credit/offer.
tactics.script: Use `polite_decline_offer`. The first offer is almost never the best one — scripts ladder. The second and third tiers are usually behind a refusal.

tactics.name: Name the script back
tactics.when: Rep is clearly still on tree, repeating value props.
tactics.script: Use `name_the_script`. Tone: friendly co-conspirator, not adversary. You're letting them know you know — gives them permission to escalate.

tactics.name: Ask for decision authority explicitly
tactics.when: You've exhausted what this rep can offer.
tactics.script: Use `ask_for_decision_authority`. Forces an honest answer about their cap — and creates a natural handoff to whoever sits above the cap.

tactics.name: Pull the unscripted-advice card
tactics.script: Use `ask_for_unscripted_advice`. Sometimes reps will tell you the off-script path ('honestly, you'd do better calling back tomorrow and asking for the loyalty team') because the script doesn't cover empathy.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep is using stilted, identical phrasing to the company's FAQ.
consider: They are reading a script — most common.
They are new and over-prepared — they may break script if asked kindly.
The call is being monitored or recorded for quality, so they're sticking close to approved language.
It's actually an IVR-assisted human, and a system is feeding suggestions onto their screen.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Ask a narrow factual question that isn't on any FAQ. Their reaction reveals which case it is.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep refuses to deviate from the menu of offers.
consider: They literally cannot — their tool has only those buttons.
They can, but you haven't given them justification to override.
They're protecting a metric — handle time, save rate, NPS.
You're talking to acquisitions when you should be talking to retention.
Their authority cap genuinely covers all reasonable cases and you're an outlier.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Ask `ask_for_decision_authority` directly. The honest answer routes you correctly.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep abruptly drops the script and becomes a normal human.
consider: You found their off-ramp and earned a candid conversation — best case.
They are about to deliver bad news and need to soften it.
They've decided to push you off the call as 'not retainable' and don't need to perform anymore.
Supervisor walked away from their desk.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Match the new register but stay on goal. This is often the moment real movement happens.

boundaries: Do NOT mock the script or the rep. Naming it ≠ ridiculing it.
Do NOT impersonate a tier or claim a status the user doesn't have ('I'm a premium customer').
Do NOT try to get the rep fired or reported — bypassing the script means going around it, not punishing the human reading it.
Do NOT keep firing out-of-script questions after they've escalated you — switch into normal-collaborator mode with the new rep.
success_signals: Rep voluntarily transfers you to retention/loyalty/supervisor.
Rep starts using phrases that aren't on the company website.
Rep types, pauses, says 'let me check on something'.
Offer escalates after a polite refusal.
failure_signals: Rep loops back to the same three offers no matter what you say.
Rep ends the call abruptly or threatens to.
Rep refuses to name what authority they have.
Hold times balloon — sign they're hoping you'll drop off.

exit_strategy.on_success: Take the escalated conversation seriously — the new rep is real authority. Drop the bypass tactics and negotiate cleanly.
exit_strategy.on_failure: End politely. Try `reset-by-callback` to roll the dice on a different rep, ideally at a different time of day.
follow_ups: Note the rep's script tells for the next call's notes.
If a specific team was promised but no transfer happened, note the rep's name and the promise.

required_user_info: What the user actually wants (the real ask).
Whether the user has authority to wait through escalations and holds.
Account-tier facts the rep will need (don't fabricate them).
